food_subgroup: Cooked vegetable dish with legume-leaf sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the combined Palak Gobi dish. FOODON_03311737 (cauliflower food product) used as closest parent for the cauliflower component; spinach curry preparation is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Palak Gobi as a composite dish. A00LC (Cauliflower, cooked) used as the closest parent anchor for the dominant vegetable component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Palak Gobi or a sufficiently close composite Indian vegetable curry dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Palak Gobi is approximately 250 g, comprising a generous portion of crispy-fried cauliflower florets coated in smooth spiced spinach puree, served as a main vegetable side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Palak (Spinach) and Cauliflower composite dish estimation; ICMR-NIN Table entries: Cauliflower (cooked) and Spinach (cooked)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Palak Gobi as a composite restaurant dish. Values estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) using a weighted recipe model: approximately 45% cauliflower (cooked), 35% spinach puree (cooked), 15% cooking oil contribution, 5% spices/aromatics. Cauliflower cooked per 100g: energy 28 kcal, protein 2.4g, fat 0.4g, carbohydrate 4.0g. Spinach cooked per 100g: energy 26 kcal, protein 2.0g, fat 0.7g, carbohydrate 2.9g. Oil addition estimated at 8g per 100g final dish based on typical Indian restaurant preparation. Final composite values calculated accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Cauliflower (cooked) and Spinach (cooked) analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Palak Gobi as a composite dish. GI estimated at 32 based on the University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked cauliflower (GI ~15, low) and cooked spinach (GI ~15, low) as the dominant carbohydrate-contributing components, weighted with the moderate oil content which further attenuates glycemic response. The composite dish GI is estimated at 32 (low category, ≤55) reflecting the low-GI vegetable base and fat-mediated gastric emptying delay. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (32 × 7.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 5.76, rounded to 5.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends in restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 250g restaurant serving of Palak Gobi contributes approximately 700 mg sodium, which represents about 30% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~32) and high dietary fibre from spinach and cauliflower
type2_diabetes.guidance: Palak Gobi has a low estimated GI of 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 5.8 per serving, making it a suitable vegetable dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat profile, high potassium, and antioxidant-rich spinach
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Palak Gobi provides a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio and is rich in potassium (~320 mg/100g) and folate (~85 mcg DFE/100g), supporting vascular health; however, the restaurant version should be monitored for total oil volume used in deep-frying the cauliflower.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Palak Gobi is a classic North Indian vegetarian dish consisting of crispy-fried or sautéed cauliflower florets (gobi) cooked in a smooth, vibrant green spinach (palak) puree seasoned with ginger, garlic, cumin, coriander, garam masala, and other aromatic spices. The dish is a staple of Indian home cooking and restaurant menus across India and the Indian diaspora in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The cauliflower is typically blanched or deep-fried before being folded into the spinach gravy, giving it a tender interior with a slightly firm bite, while the spinach sauce provides an earthy, mildly spiced, and creamy backdrop. Nutritionally, Palak Gobi is a nutrient-dense vegetable dish providing dietary fibre (approximately 2.8g/100g), iron (2.1 mg/100g), vitamin A (210 mcg RAE/100g), vitamin C (28 mg/100g), and folate (85 mcg DFE/100g) from the spinach component. The estimated glycemic index is low (~32), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free in its traditional form. Sodium content in restaurant versions can be elevated due to added salt and spice blends, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension. Palak Gobi is commonly served as a side dish alongside roti, naan, or rice in Indian cuisine contexts.
common_user_queries: Is Palak Gobi good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Palak Gobi as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN component data for cooked cauliflower and cooked spinach with an oil addition factor reflecting typical Indian restaurant deep-frying preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for the component vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is transparent, the source data is from an approved primary source (ICMR-NIN), and the component foods are well-characterised.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) using a weighted recipe model (45% cooked cauliflower, 35% cooked spinach, 15% oil, 5% spices/aromatics). Direct laboratory analysis of this dish would improve confidence to high. 2. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for these vegetable components. 3. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero for vegan plant-food preparations. 4. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as neither cauliflower nor spinach contains meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN component data. 5. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. 6. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN component data. 7. GI estimated at 32 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous values for cooked cauliflower and cooked spinach; no direct GI measurement for Palak Gobi exists in the approved GI database. 8. The restaurant version from Manam - Upper Circular Road (field survey data) is priced at SGD 20.00, suggesting a premium restaurant portion; sodium and oil content may be higher than the estimated values. 9. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no composite Indian vegetable curry codes exist in FoodOn or FoodEx2.
